1: The dating formula (baḥōdeš haššĕbîʿî, the seventh month) places the assassination just after the destruction of Jerusalem in the fifth month (ch. 39). Ishmael son of Nethaniah is identified as mizzereʿ hammelûkâ — "of royal descent" — which provides his motive: a rival claimant to Davidic authority eliminates the Babylonian-appointed governor. The ten men who accompany him suggest a planned political action, not a spontaneous crime. The MKT preserves "of royal descent" to retain the dynastic dimension of the assassination.
2: wayyāqom Yišmāʿēl ben-Nĕtanyāhû wĕhāʿăśārâ ʾănāšîm — the assassination of Gedaliah ends Israel's last chance for a viable remnant in the land. Gedaliah was of the Shaphan family (Ahikam son of Shaphan) — the family that had protected Jeremiah throughout his ministry (26:24). His death eliminates both the protective political leadership and the administrative infrastructure for covenantal life in the land. The MKT renders the action directly: "struck down Gedaliah... with the sword and killed him."
3: The wider massacre includes not only Gedaliah but "all the Jews who were with Gedaliah at Mizpah" and "the Chaldean soldiers (ʾanšê milḥāmâ mēhaKaśdîm) who were there" — the Babylonian military presence too. The elimination of the Babylonian garrison removes any protection the remnant had from Chaldean reprisal. Ishmael's action guarantees that the remnant cannot remain in the land safely.
4: The secrecy of the initial period — "before anyone knew of it" (ûbayyôm haššēnî lĕhahrōg ʾet-Gĕdalyāhû wĕʾîš lōʾ yādāʿ) — implies that Ishmael contained the news for a day. This detail prepares for the narrative trap he springs on the arriving pilgrims in v.6. The one-day concealment was the tactical window for the deception.
5: ûšĕmōnîm ʾîš bāʾû miŠĕkem miŠîlô ûmiŠōmrôn — eighty men from Shechem, Shiloh, and Samaria — all cities of the former northern kingdom. That northerners come with "shaved beards, torn clothing, and self-lacerations" (mĕgūlĕḥê zāqān ûqĕrûʿê bĕgādîm ûmitgōdĕdîm) bearing grain and incense for the temple suggests they were still oriented toward Jerusalem as their cultic center despite the northern kingdom's fall in 722 BC. The MKT preserves all three cities because they demonstrate that Israelite mourning spanned both kingdoms.
6: Ishmael's strategic deception: he goes out to meet the pilgrims bākôh bēlēk (weeping as he went), performing mourning to draw them in. The invitation — "Come to Gedaliah son of Ahikam" — is a lie that uses Gedaliah's name as bait. The MKT renders the dramatic irony of a man weeping his way to another murder by following the MT narrative exactly.
7: wayyiheî kĕbôʾām ʾel-tôk hāʿîr wayyišḥāṭēm Yišmāʿēl — the slaughter of seventy pilgrims. Wayyišlīkēm ʾel-tôk habbôr — he threw their bodies into the cistern. The scale of the atrocity (seventy killed, ten spared) and the method (thrown into a cistern) is the narrative nadir of post-fall Judah. The MKT follows the MT without softening: "slaughtered them and cast them into a pit."
8: ʾal-tĕmîtēnû kî-yeš-lānû matmōnîm baśśādeh — ten men survive by revealing hidden stores of food. Ḥiṭṭîm ûśĕʿōrîm wĕšemen ûdĕbaš (wheat, barley, oil, and honey) — agricultural survival provisions. Ishmael's economic pragmatism (he spares those with resources) shows his murders were not ideologically comprehensive. The MKT preserves the four food items as a specific detail that grounds the survival in practical terms.
9: The cistern of King Asa — habbbôr ʾăšer ʿāśâ hammelek ʾĀsāʾ — connects to the narrative of 1 Kgs 15:22 where Asa conscripted all Judah to fortify Mizpah against Baasha of Israel. A defensive cistern built centuries earlier to protect Judah now holds the bodies of murdered Judeans. The MKT preserves the historical note on Asa because it is the narrator's way of marking the horror: the city Asa built for Israel's protection now contains Israel's dead.
10: wayyišb Yišmāʿēl ʾet kol-šĕʾērît hāʿām — Ishmael takes captive "all the rest of the people in Mizpah, including the king's daughters" (ʾet-bĕnôt hammelek). The king's daughters are the last Davidic royalty in the land. Ishmael intends to take them to the Ammonites (Baalis king of Ammon was behind the conspiracy, 40:14), possibly as political pawns. The MKT renders "king's daughters" to preserve the Davidic dynastic significance.
11: Johanan son of Kareah and the other military commanders had been warned by Ishmael's intent (40:13-14) and now respond. ʾēt kol-hārāʿâ ʾăšer ʿāśâ Yišmāʿēl ben-Nĕtanyāhû — hearing of all the evil Ishmael did. The rescue mission is framed as a response to evil (rāʿâ), using the covenant-violation vocabulary.
12: The pursuit ends at hammayyim habbĕrēkâ haggedōlâ ʾăšer-bĕGibʿôn — the great pool at Gibeon. The Gibeon pool had earlier been the site of the civil conflict between Joab and Abner's forces (2 Sam 2:13). The geography carries the weight of intra-Israelite conflict. Johanan's forces "caught up with him" — the rescue is just in time.
13: The captives' relief on seeing Johanan: wayyiśmāḥû (they were overjoyed). The single word of emotional response encapsulates the shift from captivity to rescue. The MKT's "they were overjoyed" renders wayyiśmāḥû (the same root as śimḥâ, joy) — the covenantal joy-vocabulary reappearing in the darkest narrative section.
14: The captives turn back (wayyāšubû wayyēlĕkû ʾel-Yôḥānān) — using the šûb (return) vocabulary. In the wider context of Jeremiah the verb šûb carries heavy theological weight (return/repentance). Here it is a physical return, but the word choice may be the narrator's signal that this turning back is the right direction — toward Johanan rather than toward Ammon.
15: Ishmael escapes with eight men to the Ammonites (wayŷillāṭ Yišmāʿēl... weyēlek ʾel-bĕnê ʿAmmôn). His escape means he faces no earthly justice. The unresolved escape of the assassin is a narrative detail the text does not tidy up — Ishmael vanishes from the narrative as abruptly as he entered it, his crimes unpunished within the story.
16: The survivors Johanan recovered: "men of war, women, children, and court officials (sārîsîm), whom he brought back from Gibeon." The word sārîsîm (court officials, eunuchs) indicates this includes the palace infrastructure of the post-fall government. The MKT renders "court officials" as the best English equivalent for sārîsîm in this administrative context.
17: wayyēlĕkû wayyēšĕbû bĕGerût Kimhām ʾăšer ʾeṣel Bêt-Laḥem — "They set out and stayed at Geruth Chimham near Bethlehem"; Kimhām was the son of Barzillai the Gileadite (2 Sam 19:37-40) who received land near Bethlehem from David. The remnant rests on Davidic land (Bethlehem, the city of David) on their way to Egypt — the anti-Exodus direction. The MKT preserves the Bethlehem detail because it is geographically and theologically precise.
18: The stated motive for going to Egypt: fear of Chaldean reprisal (yārĕʾû mippĕnê haKaśdîm). The fear is rational — Ishmael killed the Babylonian-appointed governor. But the fear drives the decision that YHWH will explicitly forbid in ch. 42. The MKT preserves the fear motive without judgment in this verse; the judgment comes in the oracle that follows.
